01-why-multiple-agents.instruction: Give 3 real-world scenarios where a single AI agent would fail but a multi-agent system succeeds. For each, explain the specific limitation that makes one agent insufficient.
01-why-multiple-agents.hint: 1) E-commerce order fulfillment: one agent can't simultaneously check inventory, process payments, route shipping, and handle fraud detection — it would need all tools and context, exceeding practical limits. 2) Content moderation at scale: one agent can't process 10,000 posts per minute — you need parallel agents with different specializations (toxicity, spam, copyright). 3) Complex research: one agent can't hold a 50-page document plus web search results plus calculation outputs in context simultaneously.
01-why-multiple-agents.badExample: Multiple agents are better because they can do more things at once and are smarter together.
01-why-multiple-agents.goodExample: Scenario 1: E-commerce order pipeline. Single agent fails because it needs simultaneous access to inventory DB, payment gateway, shipping API, and fraud detection model — the context window can't hold all tool outputs and the latency of sequential calls is unacceptable for real-time checkout. Multi-agent: each agent owns one domain and they pipeline results. Scenario 2: Real-time content moderation at 10K posts/minute. Single agent bottleneck: can only process one post at a time. Multi-agent: pool of parallel moderators, each specialized (toxicity detector, spam classifier, copyright checker) running simultaneously. Scenario 3: Multi-source research report. Single agent's context window overflows trying to hold 20 source documents. Multi-agent: researcher agents each summarize a subset, synthesis agent combines summaries into coherent report.
01-why-multiple-agents.successMessage: Knowing when to go multi-agent is the first architecture decision. The triggers: context overflow, latency requirements, and domain specialization needs.

01-why-multiple-agents.instruction: Analyze the tradeoffs of multi-agent systems. List at least 3 advantages and 2 disadvantages compared to a single powerful agent.
01-why-multiple-agents.hint: Advantages: specialization (each agent masters its domain), parallelism (independent tasks run simultaneously), modularity (swap one agent without rewriting the system). Disadvantages: coordination overhead (agents need protocols to communicate), debugging complexity (failures span multiple agents), higher total cost (multiple LLM calls).
01-why-multiple-agents.badExample: More agents means more power. The downside is it's more complex and expensive.
01-why-multiple-agents.goodExample: Advantages: 1) Specialization — each agent has focused tools and prompts, reducing errors. A coding agent doesn't need marketing tools cluttering its context. 2) Parallelism — independent tasks execute simultaneously, cutting latency. 3) Modularity — swap the summarizer agent without touching the data-fetcher. 4) Fault isolation — one agent failing doesn't crash the pipeline. Disadvantages: 1) Coordination complexity — agents need protocols to share state, handle conflicts, and sequence work. This is new infrastructure to build and maintain. 2) Debugging difficulty — when the output is wrong, you must trace through multiple agents to find which one failed and why. 3) Higher cost — N agents = N LLM calls per task, which multiplies API costs.
01-why-multiple-agents.successMessage: The multi-agent tradeoff always comes down to: specialization gains vs. coordination costs. Choose multi-agent only when the gains clearly outweigh the overhead.

02-agent-roles-and-specialization.instruction: Design 4 specialized agent roles for an e-commerce platform. For each: what it owns, its specific tools, and how it interfaces with the other agents.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.hint: Inventory Agent: owns stock levels, tools: database queries, supplier API. Interfaces: tells Order Agent if items are available. Order Agent: owns checkout flow, tools: payment gateway, cart API. Interfaces: requests stock check from Inventory, sends confirmed orders to Shipping. Shipping Agent: owns delivery, tools: carrier APIs, tracking. Interfaces: receives orders, updates Customer Agent on delivery status. Customer Agent: owns communication, tools: email/chat API, CRM. Interfaces: receives status updates from all agents, communicates with customers.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.badExample: Agent 1 handles products, Agent 2 handles orders, Agent 3 handles shipping, Agent 4 handles customers. They share a database.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.goodExample: Agent 1 (Inventory Keeper): Owns stock levels, reorder thresholds, supplier relationships. Tools: product DB queries, supplier REST APIs, demand forecasting model. Interfaces: responds to stock-check requests from Order Agent, publishes 'low-stock' events that trigger auto-reorder. Agent 2 (Order Orchestrator): Owns checkout flow from cart to confirmation. Tools: payment gateway (Stripe), cart service, tax calculator, discount engine. Interfaces: queries Inventory for availability, sends confirmed orders to Fulfillment, notifies Customer Agent of order status. Agent 3 (Fulfillment Agent): Owns warehouse routing and shipping. Tools: warehouse management API, carrier APIs (FedEx, UPS), tracking number generator. Interfaces: receives orders from Order Agent, publishes tracking events consumed by Customer Agent. Agent 4 (Customer Concierge): Owns all customer communication. Tools: email API, chat widget, CRM, sentiment analyzer. Interfaces: subscribes to events from ALL other agents (order confirmed, shipped, delivered, out of stock) and proactively communicates with customers.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.successMessage: Clear role boundaries prevent overlap. Each agent owns a domain, has specific tools, and communicates through defined interfaces. Solid design.

02-agent-roles-and-specialization.instruction: This agent role definition is too vague: 'The helper agent assists with various tasks and can do anything needed.' Rewrite it as a properly scoped agent with clear boundaries, specific tools, and typed inputs/outputs.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.placeholder: Rewrite with specific scope, tools, and I/O...
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.hint: Pick a specific domain and define: what the agent does (and doesn't do), exactly which tools it has, what format its inputs take, what format its outputs take, and explicit boundaries.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.badExample: The task agent handles specific tasks using its specialized tools. It receives requests and returns results.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.goodExample: The Invoice Processing Agent extracts structured data from invoice documents. Tools: PDF parser, OCR engine, currency converter, company lookup API. Input: { file_path: string (PDF/PNG/JPG), expected_currency: string }. Output: { vendor_name: string, invoice_number: string, line_items: [{description, quantity, unit_price, total}], subtotal: number, tax: number, total: number, currency: 'USD', due_date: ISO-8601, confidence_score: 0-1 }. Boundaries: does NOT approve payments, does NOT contact vendors, does NOT modify any financial records. Only extracts and structures data. If confidence_score < 0.7, flags for human review instead of returning potentially wrong data.
02-agent-roles-and-specialization.successMessage: A well-defined agent has: clear scope, specific tools, typed I/O, and explicit boundaries about what it does NOT do. This prevents scope creep and makes testing straightforward.

03-communication-patterns.instruction: Compare 3 inter-agent communication patterns: direct messaging, shared blackboard, and event bus. For each: how it works, pros, cons, and a real example.
03-communication-patterns.placeholder: Pattern 1: Direct messaging — How: ... Pros: ... Cons: ... Example: ...
03-communication-patterns.hint: Direct messaging: point-to-point, Agent A calls Agent B directly. Pro: simple, low latency. Con: tight coupling, A must know about B. Example: researcher agent sends findings directly to writer agent. Shared blackboard: agents read/write to a shared state. Pro: agents don't need to know about each other. Con: contention, complex consistency. Example: multiple reviewers post critiques to shared document. Event bus: agents publish/subscribe to events. Pro: loose coupling, easy to add agents. Con: eventual consistency, harder to debug. Example: 'order.placed' triggers inventory, payment, and shipping agents.
03-communication-patterns.badExample: Direct messaging is one-to-one, blackboard is shared space, event bus is publish-subscribe. They each work differently.
03-communication-patterns.goodExample:
Direct Messaging: Agent A sends a request to Agent B and waits for response (like a function call). Pro: simple, predictable, easy to debug. Con: tight coupling — A must know B exists and its interface. Adding Agent C requires rewiring A. Example: data-cleaning agent sends sanitized CSV directly to analysis agent in a pipeline.

Shared Blackboard: agents read/write to a central state store. No agent directly contacts another. Pro: agents are decoupled — add new agents without changing existing ones. Con: race conditions on concurrent writes, harder to trace causality. Example: collaborative code review — security agent, performance agent, and style agent all post findings to a shared review document; synthesis agent reads all findings.

Event Bus: agents publish events and subscribe to topics they care about. Pro: maximally decoupled, agents can join/leave dynamically. Con: eventual consistency, event ordering challenges, harder to debug across async flows. Example: 'user.signup' event → welcome-email agent, CRM agent, and analytics agent all react independently.
03-communication-patterns.successMessage: Communication patterns are the nervous system of multi-agent architectures. The choice shapes everything: coupling, scalability, and debuggability.

03-communication-patterns.instruction: Agent A sends a JSON payload to Agent B, but B keeps crashing. Agent A output: { role: 'researcher', findings: ['item1', 'item2'], confidence: 'high' }. Agent B expects: { source_agent: string, data: array, metadata: { confidence: number } }. Error: 'TypeError: Cannot read property length of undefined'. Find all the bugs.
03-communication-patterns.hint: Three bugs: 1) Field name mismatch: A sends 'role' but B expects 'source_agent', A sends 'findings' but B expects 'data'. B looks for 'data', gets undefined, tries .length — crash. 2) Type mismatch: A sends confidence as string 'high', B expects a number. 3) Structure mismatch: A sends confidence at top level, B expects it nested in metadata object.
03-communication-patterns.badExample: The JSON format is wrong. Agent A should send what Agent B expects.
03-communication-patterns.goodExample: Bug 1 — Field name mismatch: A sends 'findings' but B looks for 'data'. B does data.length → data is undefined → TypeError. Also: 'role' vs 'source_agent' mismatch. Bug 2 — Type mismatch: A sends confidence: 'high' (string) but B expects confidence: number (e.g., 0.9). Any numeric comparison or calculation on 'high' will fail. Bug 3 — Nesting mismatch: A puts confidence at root level, B expects it nested inside metadata: { confidence: number }. B reads metadata.confidence → metadata is undefined → another crash. Fix: create a schema adapter between agents: { source_agent: msg.role, data: msg.findings, metadata: { confidence: {'high': 0.9, 'medium': 0.6, 'low': 0.3}[msg.confidence] } }. Better long-term fix: define a shared message schema (e.g., JSON Schema or Protocol Buffers) that both agents validate against.
03-communication-patterns.successMessage: Schema mismatches are the #1 cause of multi-agent failures. The fix: shared schemas or adapter layers. Always validate messages at agent boundaries.

04-orchestration-architectures.instruction: Compare centralized orchestration (one conductor agent manages all others) vs. decentralized (agents coordinate peer-to-peer). For each: how it works, when to use it, failure modes, and a real example.
04-orchestration-architectures.placeholder: Centralized: how it works... When to use... Failure modes... Decentralized: ...
04-orchestration-architectures.hint: Centralized: conductor receives task, decomposes into subtasks, assigns to workers, collects results. Use when: strict ordering needed, single point of accountability desired. Failure mode: conductor dies = everything stops. Example: ETL pipeline. Decentralized: agents discover and negotiate with peers directly. Use when: dynamic environments, agents join/leave. Failure mode: no single source of truth, hard to debug. Example: marketplace negotiations between buyer/seller agents.
04-orchestration-architectures.badExample: Centralized has a boss agent, decentralized doesn't. Centralized is simpler but has a single point of failure.
04-orchestration-architectures.goodExample:
Centralized: A conductor agent holds the execution plan, dispatches subtasks to worker agents, handles retries, and assembles final results. When to use: deterministic workflows where step order matters (ETL pipelines, document processing, CI/CD). Failure modes: conductor = single point of failure (if it crashes, no work gets dispatched); conductor becomes bottleneck under high load. Example: a data pipeline where the conductor sequences: extract → validate → transform → load, handling retries at each stage.

Decentralized: Agents advertise capabilities and negotiate work directly. No central coordinator. When to use: dynamic environments where new agents join/leave, adaptive systems (marketplace, swarm intelligence). Failure modes: no global view of system state, hard to guarantee task completion, debugging requires distributed tracing. Example: ride-sharing where driver agents and rider agents negotiate matches directly based on proximity and preferences, with no central dispatcher.
04-orchestration-architectures.successMessage: The most important multi-agent architecture decision. Centralized = control and predictability. Decentralized = flexibility and resilience.

04-orchestration-architectures.instruction: Design a hybrid orchestration architecture for an AI hiring pipeline. Some parts need centralized control (compliance, decision-making) and some benefit from decentralized execution (parallel screening). Explain your design.
04-orchestration-architectures.placeholder: The hybrid architecture uses centralized control for... and decentralized execution for...
04-orchestration-architectures.hint: Centralized conductor for the overall pipeline (ensures every candidate goes through required compliance checks in order). Decentralized parallel execution for screening (resume parser, skills matcher, culture fit analyzer all run independently on each candidate). The conductor manages sequencing and compliance gates; the workers handle the actual analysis in parallel.
04-orchestration-architectures.badExample: Use centralized for important stuff and decentralized for everything else.
04-orchestration-architectures.goodExample:
Hybrid Hiring Pipeline: A centralized Hiring Conductor manages the overall candidate journey and compliance gates. It enforces: every candidate must pass legal screening before any evaluation, all evaluations must complete before any ranking, and final decisions require human approval.

Within each pipeline stage, execution is decentralized and parallel: Stage 1 (screening): Resume Parser, Skills Matcher, and Writing Analyzer all receive the candidate data simultaneously and work independently. No coordinator needed — they publish results to a shared scoring table. Stage 2 (deep evaluation): Top 20% candidates trigger Reference Checker and Technical Assessor agents in parallel.

The conductor only steps in at gates: 'Has legal screening passed? → Release to Stage 1. Has Stage 1 completed for this candidate? → Release to Stage 2. All Stage 2 complete? → Compile ranked shortlist → Route to human reviewer.'

This gives you: compliance enforcement (centralized gates), maximum throughput (parallel workers), and auditability (conductor logs every stage transition).
04-orchestration-architectures.successMessage: Hybrid architectures give you the best of both worlds: centralized control where accountability matters, decentralized execution where speed matters.

05-shared-memory-and-state.instruction: Design a shared state system for 5 agents that need to read and write without conflicts. Cover: what store you'd use, locking strategy, consistency guarantees, and conflict resolution.
05-shared-memory-and-state.placeholder: Shared state store: ... Locking: ... Consistency: ... Conflict resolution: ...
05-shared-memory-and-state.hint: Store: PostgreSQL with row-level locking. Locking: optimistic locking with version columns — each UPDATE includes WHERE version = N, fails if another agent changed it. Consistency: read-after-write consistency for the writing agent, eventual consistency for others (acceptable 100ms lag). Conflict resolution: if two agents try to update the same record, the one with higher priority wins; the other retries with fresh data.
05-shared-memory-and-state.badExample: Use a database with locks so agents don't conflict. If there's a conflict, retry.
05-shared-memory-and-state.goodExample:
Store: PostgreSQL — battle-tested, supports row-level locking, ACID transactions, and JSON columns for flexible agent data. Schema: shared_state(id, agent_domain, key, value_json, version, updated_by, updated_at).

Locking: Optimistic locking via version column. Agent reads record (version=5), makes changes, writes with UPDATE ... WHERE version=5. If another agent changed it (version=6), the write fails cleanly — agent re-reads and retries.

Consistency: Strong consistency within transactions (agent that writes immediately sees its own changes). Other agents see updates within ~10ms (acceptable for non-real-time coordination). For real-time needs: use NOTIFY/LISTEN to push state changes to subscribing agents.

Conflict resolution: Priority-based. If two agents update the same key within 1 second: higher-priority agent's write wins (e.g., safety agent > optimization agent). Lower-priority agent gets a 'conflict' response with the winning value, re-reads, and decides whether to retry or accept. All conflicts logged to conflict_log table for analysis.
05-shared-memory-and-state.successMessage: Hardest problem in distributed systems and you've thought it through. Optimistic locking + priority-based conflict resolution is a solid production pattern.

05-shared-memory-and-state.instruction: Two agents both update the same customer record simultaneously. Agent A sets status='active' (payment confirmed). Agent B sets status='suspended' (fraud detected). The database has no locking. What goes wrong, and design a fix that ensures fraud detection always wins.
05-shared-memory-and-state.placeholder: The problem is... The fix is...
05-shared-memory-and-state.hint: Race condition: whichever writes last wins, potentially overwriting a fraud suspension with 'active' status. Fix: 1) Status hierarchy in the database — define that 'suspended' > 'active' and reject any status change that would downgrade. 2) Use a CHECK constraint or trigger that prevents moving from 'suspended' to 'active' without explicit admin override.
05-shared-memory-and-state.badExample: Add a lock so only one agent can write at a time. Problem solved.
05-shared-memory-and-state.goodExample:
Problem: race condition. If Agent A writes status='active' AFTER Agent B writes status='suspended', the fraud detection is silently overwritten. The customer continues transacting despite detected fraud — a dangerous data integrity bug.

Fix — status hierarchy with database enforcement:
1) Define status priority: suspended(3) > under_review(2) > active(1) > pending(0)
2) Database trigger: BEFORE UPDATE, check IF NEW.status_priority < OLD.status_priority THEN RAISE EXCEPTION 'Cannot downgrade status from % to %'. This makes it physically impossible to move from 'suspended' to 'active' at the database level.
3) Agent A's payment confirmation attempts to set 'active', but if fraud was already detected, the trigger blocks it and returns an error. Agent A then reads the current status, sees 'suspended', and halts the payment flow.
4) Admin override: a separate 'clear_fraud_flag' function that requires human authentication can move status from 'suspended' to 'active' — but no agent can do it autonomously.

This is defense in depth: the safety rule lives in the database, not in agent code that could have bugs.
05-shared-memory-and-state.successMessage: Race conditions in shared state are the silent killer. Database-level enforcement means safety rules can't be bypassed by buggy agent code.

06-conflict-resolution.instruction: Design a voting protocol for 3 agents that disagree on a content moderation decision. Include: how votes are weighted, tie-breaking mechanism, confidence thresholds, and when to trigger human override.
06-conflict-resolution.placeholder: Voting protocol: ... Weights: ... Tie-breaking: ... Human override: ...
06-conflict-resolution.hint: Each agent votes (allow/flag/block) with a confidence score (0-1). Weighted by specialty — toxicity detector gets 1.5x weight for explicit content. Tie-breaking: if no majority after weighting, escalate to human. Human override: if any agent votes 'block' with confidence > 0.95, auto-block regardless (safety override). All decisions logged for appeals.
06-conflict-resolution.badExample: Three agents vote and majority wins. If they disagree, a human decides.
06-conflict-resolution.goodExample:
Voting Protocol:
Each agent submits: { decision: 'allow'|'flag'|'block', confidence: 0.0-1.0, reasoning: string }

Weighting: Toxicity Detector = 1.5x weight for explicit content. Context Analyzer = 1.5x for satire/nuance edge cases. Free Speech Advocate = 1.0x baseline. Weights are topic-dependent.

Resolution: Weighted majority wins if all confidences > 0.7. If any agent's confidence < 0.7, multiply all votes by their confidence scores and take weighted majority.

Safety override: If Toxicity Detector votes 'block' with confidence > 0.95, auto-block regardless of other votes. Safety first.

Tie-breaking: If no weighted majority (e.g., all three choose different decisions), escalate to human moderator with all three reasonings displayed.

Human override trigger: 1) Three-way deadlock. 2) Any agent flags 'edge case — high uncertainty.' 3) Content involves public figures or minors.

Audit: every decision logged with all votes, weights, and final outcome. Appeals process re-evaluates with fresh agent instances plus original reasoning.
06-conflict-resolution.successMessage: Democratic agent decisions with safety overrides and human escalation. Responsible design that scales.

06-conflict-resolution.instruction: The Speed Agent wants to cache all API responses for 1 hour. The Freshness Agent wants zero caching for real-time data. Design a resolution strategy that doesn't just pick one winner but finds a nuanced solution.
06-conflict-resolution.hint: Classify data by volatility: static data (1-hour cache), semi-volatile (5-minute cache), real-time (no cache). Use a Mediator Agent that applies these rules. Allow either agent to escalate with a priority flag for edge cases.
06-conflict-resolution.badExample: Split the difference and cache for 30 minutes. Or let the user configure caching per endpoint.
06-conflict-resolution.goodExample:
Resolution: data-classification-based caching policy.

Step 1 — Classify every endpoint: Static (product descriptions, about pages) = 1-hour cache (Speed Agent wins). Semi-volatile (user profiles, preferences) = 5-minute cache (compromise). Real-time (stock prices, inventory counts, live chat) = zero cache (Freshness Agent wins).

Step 2 — A Policy Mediator Agent maintains the classification table. Both Speed and Freshness agents submit their recommendations; the Mediator applies the classification rules.

Step 3 — Escalation: either agent can tag a specific request with 'critical-freshness' (force cache-bust) or 'critical-performance' (force cache) to temporarily override the default for that request.

Step 4 — Learning: log cache hit rates and data staleness metrics weekly. If cached data is causing stale reads > 5% of the time for a 'static' endpoint, reclassify it to 'semi-volatile.'

Result: neither agent 'wins' — the system gets context-appropriate caching that evolves based on actual data behavior.
06-conflict-resolution.successMessage: The best conflict resolution doesn't pick a winner — it finds the nuanced answer that both agents were approximating. Data classification turns a binary choice into a spectrum.

07-scaling-agent-systems.instruction: Your multi-agent system handles 100 tasks/day and needs to scale to 10,000/day. Identify 4 bottlenecks that will break and describe the architectural change needed for each.
07-scaling-agent-systems.hint: 1) Single orchestrator can't dispatch 10K tasks → multiple orchestrator instances with work queues. 2) Shared database contention → partition by tenant/type, add read replicas. 3) LLM API rate limits → tiered models (local for simple, API for complex). 4) Sequential processing → parallel agent pools with auto-scaling based on queue depth.
07-scaling-agent-systems.badExample: Add more agents and bigger servers. Also get faster API access.
07-scaling-agent-systems.goodExample:
Bottleneck 1 — Orchestrator throughput: single conductor can't dispatch 10K tasks. Fix: replace single orchestrator with a distributed work queue (Redis/SQS). Multiple orchestrator instances pull from the queue. Consistent hashing routes related tasks to the same instance.

Bottleneck 2 — Database contention: all agents hitting one Postgres instance. Fix: shard state by customer ID or task type. Add read replicas for agents that only query. Cache hot data in Redis (TTL 60s).

Bottleneck 3 — LLM API rate limits: 10K tasks/day = ~7/minute, but bursts hit rate limits. Fix: model router — simple tasks use a local model (Llama 3), complex tasks use Claude API. Batch similar requests. Implement token bucket rate limiter with retry queue.

Bottleneck 4 — Sequential processing: agents process one task at a time. Fix: create agent pools — 5 instances of each agent type, auto-scaling based on queue depth. When queue > 50, spin up more instances. When queue < 10, scale down. Stateless agent design enables horizontal scaling.
07-scaling-agent-systems.successMessage: 100 to 10,000 requires rethinking every component. Every bottleneck at small scale becomes a crisis at 100x.

07-scaling-agent-systems.instruction: Write a graceful degradation strategy for when your multi-agent system is overloaded. Define 3 priority tiers of agents and what happens to each tier under load.
07-scaling-agent-systems.hint: Tier 1 (never disable): safety, authentication, payment processing. Tier 2 (reduce capacity): personalization, analytics, recommendations — run at 50% throughput. Tier 3 (shed first): A/B testing, enrichment, non-essential notifications — pause entirely under load. Implement circuit breakers: if error rate > 40%, trip the breaker and use fallback.
07-scaling-agent-systems.badExample: When overloaded, queue everything and process slowly. Don't disable anything important.
07-scaling-agent-systems.goodExample:
Graceful Degradation Strategy:

Tier 1 — CRITICAL (never disable): Safety moderation, authentication, payment processing, data integrity checks. Under load: these get 100% of available resources. Other tiers shed before these feel any impact.

Tier 2 — IMPORTANT (reduce, don't remove): Personalization agent, search ranking, recommendation engine. Under load: switch to cached/precomputed results instead of real-time computation. Serve yesterday's recommendations instead of fresh ones. Response quality drops but service continues.

Tier 3 — NICE-TO-HAVE (shed first): A/B testing agent, analytics enrichment, non-critical notifications, content pre-warming. Under load: pause entirely. These tasks queue and process when load normalizes.

Circuit breakers: any agent with error rate > 40% over 60 seconds trips its breaker — traffic routes to a static fallback (template responses, cached data). Auto-recovery: when load drops below threshold for 5 minutes, restore tiers in reverse order (T3 last).

Model downgrade: under extreme load, swap Claude API calls for a local Llama model. Lower quality but maintains throughput.
07-scaling-agent-systems.successMessage: Graceful degradation is how you build reliable systems. The pattern: priority tiers, circuit breakers, cached fallbacks, and automatic recovery.

08-human-oversight-patterns.instruction: Design 3 levels of human oversight for an AI system: full autonomy, approval required, and human-in-the-loop. For each level, specify exactly what triggers it using concrete thresholds.
08-human-oversight-patterns.placeholder: Level 1 (Full autonomy): triggers when... Level 2 (Approval): triggers when... Level 3 (Human-in-loop): triggers when...
08-human-oversight-patterns.hint: Full autonomy: all agents agree with confidence > 0.9 AND action is reversible AND blast radius < 10 users. Approval required: any action affecting > 100 users, spending > $500, or confidence < 0.7. Human-in-the-loop: irreversible decisions (account deletion, legal responses), spending > $5000, or agent conflict after 3 resolution attempts.
08-human-oversight-patterns.badExample: Low-risk stuff is autonomous, medium-risk needs approval, high-risk needs a human. Categorize tasks by risk level.
08-human-oversight-patterns.goodExample:
Level 1 — Full Autonomy (no human): Triggers when ALL of: agent confidence > 0.9, action is fully reversible within 24h, affects < 10 users, financial impact < $50. Examples: categorizing support tickets, sending standard acknowledgment emails, updating internal dashboards, routine data cleanup.

Level 2 — Approval Required (human reviews, then agent executes): Triggers when ANY of: action affects 10-1000 users, financial impact $50-$5000, confidence 0.6-0.9, content will be published externally. Process: agent prepares action + reasoning → human dashboard shows 3-option UI (Approve/Reject/Modify) → 5-minute timeout defaults to reject (conservative). Examples: sending bulk emails, adjusting pricing, publishing blog posts.

Level 3 — Human-in-the-Loop (human decides, agent assists): Triggers when ANY of: irreversible action (account deletion, legal response, contract signing), affects > 1000 users, financial impact > $5000, agent conflict unresolved after 3 rounds, involves PII or regulated data. Process: agent presents analysis + recommendation → human makes decision → agent executes exactly what human specified. Examples: terminating user accounts, responding to legal threats, changing security policies.
08-human-oversight-patterns.successMessage: Graduated oversight is how you deploy autonomous systems responsibly. Concrete thresholds make it testable and auditable.

08-human-oversight-patterns.instruction: Write 5 concrete, testable rules for when a multi-agent system MUST escalate to a human. Rules should be specific enough to implement as code, not vague guidelines.
08-human-oversight-patterns.placeholder: Rule 1: ESCALATE when... Rule 2: ESCALATE when...
08-human-oversight-patterns.hint: Rules should reference specific measurable conditions: confidence scores, dollar amounts, user counts, time thresholds, error counts. Not 'when it's important' but 'when confidence < 0.6 on a decision affecting > 100 users.'
08-human-oversight-patterns.badExample: 1. Escalate when unsure. 2. Escalate for important decisions. 3. Always escalate for money stuff. 4. Escalate when agents disagree. 5. Escalate for legal issues.
08-human-oversight-patterns.goodExample:
Rule 1: ESCALATE when any agent's confidence < 0.6 on a decision that affects money, access permissions, or personal data. Test: if (decision.confidence < 0.6 && decision.category in ['financial', 'access', 'pii']) → escalate.

Rule 2: ESCALATE when conflict resolution fails after 3 rounds. If agents still disagree after 3 voting/mediation cycles, the problem is genuinely ambiguous. Test: if (conflict.resolution_attempts >= 3 && conflict.resolved === false) → escalate.

Rule 3: AUTO-HANDLE when all agents agree (confidence > 0.85) AND action is reversible within 24h. Test: if (all_agents.min_confidence > 0.85 && action.reversible_hours <= 24) → auto-execute.

Rule 4: ESCALATE when blast radius > 100 users OR financial impact > $1000. Scale amplifies mistakes. Test: if (action.affected_users > 100 || action.financial_impact_usd > 1000) → escalate.

Rule 5: ESCALATE when an agent detects its own output anomaly — e.g., generating 10x more actions than historical average, or accessing resources it normally doesn't touch. Test: if (agent.action_rate > historical_avg * 10 || agent.accessed_new_resources) → escalate + self-suspend.
08-human-oversight-patterns.successMessage: Five rules you could implement as code today. Concrete thresholds, testable conditions, and the anomaly self-detection in Rule 5 is especially important for catching compromised agents.

09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.instruction: Write a detailed prompt for Claude to design a multi-agent customer support system. Include requirements for: agent roles, escalation paths, knowledge sharing between agents, and quality metrics.
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.placeholder: Design a multi-agent customer support system that...
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.hint: Roles: triage agent (classifies and routes), billing specialist, technical support, account manager. Escalation: triage → specialist → senior specialist → human. Knowledge sharing: shared customer context that follows the ticket. Metrics: first-contact resolution rate, average handle time, customer satisfaction score, escalation rate.
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.badExample: Design a customer support system with multiple AI agents that handle different types of questions and can escalate to humans.
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.goodExample:
Design a multi-agent customer support system for a SaaS platform with 50,000 users. Requirements:

Agent Roles: 1) Triage Agent — classifies incoming tickets by type (billing, technical, account, feature request) and urgency (P1-P4). Routes to the appropriate specialist. 2) Billing Agent — handles subscription changes, payment failures, refund requests. Tools: Stripe API, billing database. 3) Technical Agent — debugs user issues, checks system status, provides workarounds. Tools: error log search, system health API, documentation RAG. 4) Account Agent — handles account settings, data exports, deletion requests. Tools: user admin API, audit log.

Escalation: If any specialist agent confidence < 0.7 OR customer sentiment turns negative (detected by tone analyzer) → escalate to senior agent (higher-capability model with full context). If senior can't resolve in 3 exchanges → route to human with full conversation + agent analysis.

Knowledge sharing: customer context object travels with every handoff: {customer_id, plan, tenure, previous_tickets, sentiment_trajectory, actions_taken_this_session}. No agent starts from scratch.

Metrics: First-contact resolution > 70%, average handle time < 5 minutes, CSAT > 4.2/5, escalation to human < 15%, resolution accuracy > 90% (sampled weekly).
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.successMessage: Customer support is the perfect multi-agent use case: clear specializations, natural escalation paths, and measurable quality metrics.

09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.instruction: Choose a real industry (healthcare, logistics, finance, etc.) and analyze how a multi-agent system could transform a specific workflow. Identify 4 agent roles, their data flows, and what makes this better than the current manual process.
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.placeholder: Industry: ... Workflow: ... Agent 1: ... Data flows: ... Why it's better: ...
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.hint: Pick a specific workflow, not a vague industry application. E.g., 'hospital emergency room triage' not 'healthcare.' Define concrete agents with specific tools and data they exchange. The 'why it's better' should cite specific improvements: time saved, errors reduced, capacity increased.
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.badExample: In healthcare, agents could help with patient care. One reads records, one suggests treatments, one handles billing.
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.goodExample:
Industry: Logistics. Workflow: International shipping container routing (currently takes 2-3 hours of manual coordination per shipment).

Agent 1 (Booking Agent): receives shipping requests, validates cargo specs against carrier requirements, books container space. Tools: carrier API, cargo classifier, rate calculator. Output: booking confirmation with vessel/voyage ID.

Agent 2 (Customs Agent): pre-fills customs declarations per destination country, flags restricted goods, attaches required certificates. Tools: trade compliance database, document generator, HS code classifier. Output: customs package per port of call.

Agent 3 (Route Optimizer): monitors real-time port congestion, weather, fuel costs across all shipping lanes. Tools: AIS vessel tracking API, weather API, port congestion feeds. Output: optimal route with ETAs per port.

Agent 4 (Exception Handler): monitors for disruptions (port strikes, weather delays, equipment failure). Tools: news feed monitor, carrier status API, alternative route calculator. Output: re-routing recommendations + customer notifications.

Data flow: Booking → Customs (needs cargo details) → Route Optimizer (needs ports of call) → all feed into Exception Handler (monitors everything). Shared state: shipment record in PostgreSQL updated by all agents.

Why better: reduces coordination from 2-3 hours to 5 minutes per shipment. Customs compliance errors drop from ~12% to < 1% (automated validation). Route optimization saves 8-15% on fuel costs. Exception handling is proactive (agent detects delay before customer notices) vs. reactive (human scrambles after the fact).
09-real-world-multi-agent-systems.successMessage: A concrete, production-ready system design. You specified agents, data flows, tools, and quantified the improvement over manual processes.

10-building-your-agent-team.instruction: Final project: design a complete multi-agent system for a project you care about. Include: system name, 4+ agent roster with roles/tools, communication pattern, shared state design, oversight rules, monitoring strategy, and deployment plan.
10-building-your-agent-team.placeholder: System name: ... Agent roster: ... Communication: ... State: ... Oversight: ... Monitoring: ... Deploy: ...
10-building-your-agent-team.hint: Pick something real to you — personal productivity, content creation, small business automation, learning system. Make it concrete enough that you could start building it tomorrow. Cover all the architecture decisions from the course: roles, communication, state, conflicts, oversight, scaling, monitoring.
10-building-your-agent-team.badExample: I'd build a system with 4 agents that each handle different parts of my project. They'd communicate through an event bus and use a shared database.
10-building-your-agent-team.goodExample:
System: ContentForge — automated content pipeline for a solo creator.

Agent 1 (Scout): monitors RSS feeds, Twitter trends, and Reddit for topics in my niche. Tools: RSS parser, Twitter API, Reddit API, relevance classifier. Publishes 'topic.discovered' events.

Agent 2 (Researcher): triggered by Scout's topics. Deep-dives: finds sources, extracts key data, identifies unique angles. Tools: web search, document summarizer, fact-checker. Output: structured research brief to shared state.

Agent 3 (Writer): takes research brief, produces draft matching my voice (fine-tuned on my past content). Tools: writing LLM, style guide RAG, outline generator. Output: draft article + metadata.

Agent 4 (Publisher): reviews draft quality score, formats for each platform (blog, newsletter, social), schedules publication. Tools: CMS API, email API, social APIs, image generator for thumbnails.

Agent 5 (Analyst): tracks published content performance. Tools: analytics APIs, engagement trackers. Feeds insights back to Scout (what topics perform well) and Writer (what styles get engagement).

Communication: event bus (Kafka). Events: topic.discovered, research.complete, draft.ready, published, performance.report.
Shared state: PostgreSQL — content_pipeline table tracks each piece through stages with status and metadata.
Oversight: Writer's drafts require confidence > 0.8 to auto-publish. Below 0.8 → queued for human review. Any content mentioning people/companies → always human review.
Monitoring: Grafana dashboard with per-agent throughput, error rate, queue depth. Alert if any agent stalls > 1 hour.
Deploy: Docker containers per agent on a $20/mo VPS. Start with 1 instance each, scale Writer and Publisher as content volume grows.
10-building-your-agent-team.successMessage: Graduate-level AI architecture designed from scratch. Every concept from the course — roles, communication, state, oversight, monitoring — applied to a real system you could build.

10-building-your-agent-team.instruction: Write a pre-build checklist of 8 questions you must answer before building any multi-agent system. These should be the critical design decisions that determine success or failure.
10-building-your-agent-team.placeholder: 1. Does this task actually need... 2. What are the minimum...
10-building-your-agent-team.hint: Cover: necessity (does it need multiple agents?), roles (minimum viable team), communication (what pattern?), state (how shared?), failures (what happens when agents break?), oversight (where do humans fit?), testing (how do you validate?), scaling (what breaks at 10x?).
10-building-your-agent-team.badExample: 1. How many agents? 2. What do they do? 3. How do they talk? 4. What if something breaks? 5. How do you test it?
10-building-your-agent-team.goodExample:
Multi-Agent Pre-Build Checklist:

1) Does this genuinely need multiple agents, or can a single agent with multiple tools handle it? (The simplest architecture that works is the right one.)

2) What is the minimum viable agent team? What's the smallest number of agents that solves the core problem? (Start with 2-3, not 10.)

3) What communication pattern fits: pipeline (sequential steps), fan-out (parallel independent tasks), or event-driven (loosely coupled reactions)?

4) How is state shared between agents — database, message payloads, or event log — and how do you handle concurrent write conflicts?

5) What is the failure strategy for each agent? Can it retry, fall back, skip, or must it escalate? What happens to downstream agents when one fails?

6) Where are the human oversight checkpoints? What decisions are too consequential for full automation? What are the concrete escalation thresholds?

7) How do you test this end-to-end? Can you simulate realistic multi-agent interactions including failure scenarios, not just happy paths?

8) At what scale does this architecture break, and what's the migration plan? If you 10x the load, which component fails first?
10-building-your-agent-team.successMessage: This checklist captures every critical design decision. Use it at the start of every multi-agent project — it'll prevent the most common architectural mistakes.
